**Must demonstrate inability to full-pay before CSED!**

sections.title: Currently Not Collectible (CNC)
sections.content:
**When taxpayer truly cannot pay:**

• All income needed for necessary expenses
• No ability to make any payment
• Collection suspended (not forgiven)
• CSED continues to run

**Requirements:**
• Full financial disclosure (Form 433-A/B)
• IRS determines inability to pay
• Periodic review of status

**Wait for CSED expiration if tax can't be resolved!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Protecting the Client During Collection
sections.content:
**While working on alternative:**

• Get levy released if causing hardship
• Request Collection Due Process hearing if lien/levy issued
• Don't let statute of limitations expire on requesting relief
• File all required returns (IRS won't work with non-filers)
• Stay current on new liabilities

**Collection doesn't stop automatically - must request!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Guaranteed IA: <$10,000, must accept
Streamlined: <$50,000, no financials, 72 months
OIC: Settle for less, must show can't full-pay
CNC: Collection suspended, CSED runs
All options require filed returns
Stay current on new taxes during agreement

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer owes $48,000 in back taxes, including penalties and interest. They have filed all required returns, are currently compliant, and can afford to pay $667 per month. Which collection alternative is MOST suitable for this taxpayer?
options: Offer in Compromise based on Doubt as to Collectibility
Streamlined Installment Agreement
Currently Not Collectible (CNC) Status
Offer in Compromise based on Effective Tax Administration
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A Streamlined Installment Agreement is the best option because the taxpayer owes less than $50,000, can pay off the debt within 72 months ($667 x 72 = $48,024), and is currently compliant. An OIC is unnecessary as they can afford to pay the full amount. CNC is inappropriate as they have the ability to pay.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the taxpayer's ability to pay and the amount owed.

reveal.question: What is the basic formula used by the IRS to determine the minimum acceptable amount for an Offer in Compromise?
reveal.answer: Offer = RCP (Reasonable Collection Potential). RCP is calculated as Future Income + Asset Equity.

rows.label: Financial Disclosure
values: Required (Form 433-A/B)
Streamlined IA: Not Required

rows.label: Payment Amount
values: $0 - All income needed for necessary expenses
Negotiated monthly payment

rows.label: CSED Impact
values: CSED continues to run
CSED continues to run

rows.label: Compliance
values: Taxpayer must demonstrate inability to pay with full financial disclosure
Taxpayer must be current with filing and payment requirements going forward

title: Collection Due Process and Appeals
description: Understand taxpayer rights to challenge collection actions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Collection Due Process (CDP) gives taxpayers the right to an independent hearing before (or after) liens and levies! This is a powerful protection you need to know.

sections.title: When CDP Rights Arise
sections.content:
**Federal Tax Lien (LT11/Letter 3172):**
• Right to CDP hearing
• 30 days to request after notice

**Levy (LTR 1058/LT11):**
• Right to CDP hearing
• 30 days to request after notice

**First lien/levy triggers CDP rights.**

Subsequent liens/levies: equivalent hearing only.

sections.title: What Can Be Raised at CDP
content: Appropriateness of collection action
Collection alternatives (IA, OIC, CNC)
Spousal defenses
Underlying tax liability (if no prior opportunity)
Procedural errors by IRS
Whether lien/levy meets legal requirements
items: **Appropriateness of Collection**: Challenge if the levy or lien is appropriate under the circumstances.
**Spousal Defenses**: Innocent spouse relief claims.
**Collection Alternatives**: Offer in Compromise (OIC) or Installment Agreement (IA).
**Underlying Liability**: Can only dispute the tax liability itself if the taxpayer did not receive a statutory notice of deficiency or otherwise have an opportunity to dispute it generally.

sections.title: CDP Process
sections.content:
**Steps:**

1. Receive CDP notice (LT11, Letter 3172, etc.)
2. File Form 12153 within 30 days
3. Case assigned to Settlement Officer
4. Hearing (phone, in-person, or correspondence)
5. Determination letter issued
6. 30 days to petition Tax Court if disagree

**Collection suspended during CDP (except for collection period)!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Critical Deadlines
sections.content:
**MUST file Form 12153 within 30 days for:**

• Collection suspension
• Tax Court review rights
• Statute tolling

**After 30 days:**
• Only equivalent hearing available
• Collection continues
• No Tax Court review

**Calendar this immediately upon receipt of CDP notice!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CDP: 30 days to request, suspends collection
Equivalent hearing: After 30 days, no suspension
Use Form 12153 for both types
Can raise collection alternatives at hearing
Underlying liability: only if no prior opportunity
Tax Court review only for timely CDP request

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following actions by the IRS triggers Collection Due Process (CDP) rights, entitling a taxpayer to a CDP hearing?
options: Issuance of a notice of deficiency.
The first notice of a Federal Tax Lien filing (LT11/Letter 3172).
A notice of intent to levy on a taxpayer's state tax refund offset.
A notice of adjustment to the taxpayer's account.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The first notice of a Federal Tax Lien filing (LT11/Letter 3172) triggers CDP rights, giving the taxpayer 30 days to request a hearing. A notice of intent to levy also triggers CDP rights, but only the first levy. Subsequent levies only allow for equivalent hearings.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which actions directly involve seizing a taxpayer's property or rights to property.

reveal.question: What is the deadline to file Form 12153 to request a Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing after receiving a CDP notice?
reveal.answer: The deadline to file Form 12153 is within 30 days of the date of the CDP notice (LT11, Letter 3172, etc.).

rows.label: Trigger
values: First Federal Tax Lien or Levy
Subsequent Liens or Levies

rows.label: Appeal to Tax Court
values: Tax Court Petition Allowed (within 30 days of Determination Letter)
No Tax Court Petition Allowed

rows.label: Collection Suspension
values: Collection generally suspended during the hearing process
Collection is NOT suspended during the hearing process

title: Statute of Limitations
description: Master the time limits on assessments, refunds, and collections

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Statutes of limitation protect both taxpayers and the IRS. Knowing when time runs out on assessments, refunds, and collections is critical to effective representation and planning.

sections.title: Three Key Statutes
rows: Later of due date or filing date
3 years from filing OR 2 years from payment

sections.title: Assessment Statute (ASED)
sections.content:
**General Rule: 3 years** from later of due date or filing date.

**Extended to 6 years:**
• Omission of gross income > 25% of reported income
• Certain foreign income items

**No Limit (Unlimited):**
• Fraud
• Willful attempt to evade tax
• Failure to file a return

**Extended by Agreement:**
Form 872 (fixed date) or 872-A (open-ended) extends ASED.

sections.title: Refund Statute
sections.content:
**Must claim refund by LATER of:**

• 3 years from date return was filed, OR
• 2 years from date tax was paid

**Amount Limited:**
• If claiming within 3 years: Can get back what was paid within 3 years + extensions
• If claiming within 2 years (but after 3 years from filing): Only what was paid in prior 2 years

**Key:** Missing the deadline = losing the refund forever!

sections.title: Collection Statute (CSED)
sections.content:
**10 years from date of assessment.**

After CSED:
• Tax is legally uncollectible
• Liens release automatically
• Cannot levy or seize property

**CSED Suspended:**
• During bankruptcy
• While OIC pending (+ 30 days)
• While CDP appeal pending (+ 30 days)
• While Innocent Spouse request pending
• Time taxpayer is out of country (6+ months)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Form 872 vs 872-A
sections.content:
**Form 872:** Fixed ending date
• Specifies a particular date ASED expires
• Cannot be revoked
• Plan for specific date

**Form 872-A:** Open-ended
• No specific ending date
• Remains open until terminated
• Either party can terminate (90 days + 60 days notice)

**Key:** Open-ended 872-A can leave case open indefinitely!

sections.title: Protective Refund Claims
sections.content:
**When to File:**
• Statute about to expire
• Issue pending that might affect refund
• Hedge against possible later development

**How to File:**
• Form 1040-X or formal claim
• State specific basis for claim
• Reference pending case/ruling if applicable

**Preserves right to refund** even if issue not yet resolved.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Assessment statute: 3 years (6 years for 25%+ omission, unlimited for fraud)
Refund statute: 3 years from filing OR 2 years from payment
Collection statute: 10 years from assessment
Form 872 extends ASED to fixed date; 872-A is open-ended
Certain events suspend collection statute (bankruptcy, OIC, CDP)
Protective claims preserve refund rights when statute running
No return filed = no statute starts running on assessment

sections.title: Assessment Statute of Limitations - Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer filed their 2022 income tax return on March 15, 2023. They inadvertently omitted $60,000 of gross income. Their return reported a total gross income of $180,000. What is the last date the IRS can assess additional tax for the 2022 tax year?
options: April 15, 2026
March 15, 2029
April 15, 2029
Unlimited - there is no statute of limitations
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The general rule is 3 years from the later of the due date or filing date. However, since the taxpayer omitted gross income exceeding 25% of the reported gross income ($60,000 > 25% of $180,000 = $45,000), the statute of limitations is extended to 6 years from the later of the due date (April 15, 2023) or filing date (March 15, 2023). Therefore, the assessment statute expires on March 15, 2029.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the extended assessment statute rule for omissions of gross income exceeding 25%.

reveal.question: What are the two possible deadlines for filing a claim for a refund, and which one takes precedence?
reveal.answer: A taxpayer must file a claim for refund by the LATER of: (1) 3 years from the date the return was filed, OR (2) 2 years from the date the tax was paid. The later date is controlling.

knowledgeCheck.question: The IRS assessed a tax liability against John on June 1, 2020. On August 1, 2029, John filed an Offer in Compromise (OIC). The IRS rejected the OIC on December 1, 2029. When does the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) occur?
options: June 1, 2030
February 1, 2030
October 1, 2030
December 31, 2030
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The collection statute is generally 10 years from the date of assessment (June 1, 2020 + 10 years = June 1, 2030). However, it is suspended while an OIC is pending, plus 30 days. The OIC was pending from August 1, 2029, to December 1, 2029 (4 months). Therefore, the CSED is extended by 4 months + 30 days, making the new CSED October 1, 2030.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to add 30 days to the end of the suspension period when calculating the extended CSED.

title: Office and Field Examinations
description: Master in-person audit preparation and representation strategies

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: In-person audits require face-time with IRS examiners. Your preparation and representation skills directly impact outcomes. Know how to prepare clients, handle document requests, and navigate the examination process.

sections.title: Pre-Audit Preparation
sections.content:
**Before the Examination:**

1. **Review the Return**
   • Identify potential issues before IRS does
   • Understand each item that may be questioned
   • Locate all supporting documentation

2. **Gather Documentation**
   • Organize by issue and year
   • Make copies - never give originals
   • Identify gaps and address them

3. **Interview the Client**
   • Understand facts behind each item
   • Identify potential problem areas
   • Prepare client for possible questions

sections.title: Office Audit Procedures
sections.content:
**Conducted at IRS Office:**

**Initial Contact:**
• IRS sends appointment letter
• Lists items to be examined
• Specifies documents to bring

**At the Audit:**
• Present only requested documents
• Answer only questions asked
• Don't volunteer information
• Take notes on discussions
• Request breaks if needed

**Your Role as Representative:**
• Attend without client (if permitted and appropriate)
• Control information flow
• Advocate for client's positions

sections.title: Field Audit Procedures
sections.content:
**Conducted at Taxpayer's Location:**

**Why Field Audit?**
• Business examinations
• Complex returns
• Need to inspect books and records on-site
• High-income individuals

**Controlling the Environment:**
• Designate conference room away from operations
• Keep other records out of sight
• Have records organized and ready
• Don't let examiner wander freely

**Revenue Agent vs. Revenue Officer:**
• Revenue Agent: Conducts examinations
• Revenue Officer: Collections (different function)

sections.title: Information Document Requests (IDRs)
sections.content:
**IDR Process:**

• Written request from examiner for documents
• Specifies items needed and deadline
• Must respond timely

**Best Practices:**
• Request clarification if unclear
• Negotiate deadlines if needed
• Provide ONLY what's requested
• Document what you provide
• Keep copies of everything submitted

**Extensions:** Usually granted if requested before deadline

sections.title: ⚠️ Common Audit Mistakes
sections.content:
**Avoid These Errors:**

• Volunteering extra information
• Letting client speak without preparation
• Providing documents not requested
• Missing IDR deadlines
• Being confrontational with examiner
• Failing to document what was discussed

**Remember:** Examiners can expand scope if they find issues you didn't expect!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Office audits at IRS; field audits at taxpayer location
Thorough preparation is critical before any examination
Provide only documents specifically requested
Respond to IDRs timely - request extensions if needed
Representative can often attend without client
Document all discussions and items provided
Don't volunteer information beyond what's asked

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following actions is MOST important for a tax professional to take *before* an IRS office or field examination?
options: Contact the IRS agent to establish a personal rapport.
Review the tax return in question and gather all supporting documentation.
Prepare a detailed defense of every item on the tax return, regardless of its complexity.
Advise the client to amend the tax return to correct any potential errors, even if insignificant.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Reviewing the return and gathering documentation is crucial for identifying potential issues and preparing a defense. While amending might be necessary in some cases, and rapport is helpful, the core preparation involves understanding and substantiating the return as filed. Option A is inappropriate. Option C is excessive, and Option D is premature without proper review.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the primary goal of pre-audit preparation: understanding and supporting the return.

reveal.question: During an office audit, what is the BEST practice regarding the information you provide to the IRS auditor?
reveal.answer: Present only the documents specifically requested and answer only the questions asked. Avoid volunteering additional information.

sections.title: Office vs. Field Audit: Key Differences

rows.label: Location
values: IRS Office
Taxpayer's Business or Home

rows.label: Common Focus
values: Specific items on the return
Business examinations, complex returns, high-income individuals

rows.label: Document Presentation
values: Bring requested documents to the IRS office.
Maintain control over documents at the taxpayer's location.

knowledgeCheck.question: In a field audit, which of the following is the MOST effective strategy for controlling the audit environment?
options: Allow the IRS agent unrestricted access to all areas of the business.
Designate a conference room away from daily business operations and keep unrelated records out of sight.
Provide the IRS agent with a detailed explanation of all business processes, even if not directly related to the items under examination.
Offer the IRS agent complimentary meals and beverages to foster a positive relationship.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Designating a separate room and limiting access to relevant documents helps control the scope of the audit and prevents unnecessary scrutiny. Unrestricted access (Option A) is unwise. Over-explanation (Option C) can create issues. While being polite is good, focusing on unrelated hospitality (Option D) isn't the primary goal.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider how to limit the scope of the audit to only what is necessary.

title: Information Document Requests
description: Master responding to IRS document requests and protecting privileges

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: IDRs are the IRS's formal method of requesting documents during an examination. How you respond affects the audit's scope, duration, and outcome. Know how to respond strategically and protect privileges.

sections.title: What is an IDR?
sections.content:
**Information Document Request (Form 4564):**

• Formal written request for documents
• Lists specific items needed
• Provides response deadline (usually 10-30 days)
• Part of the permanent examination file

**Types of Requests:**
• Documents (bank statements, receipts, contracts)
• Information (explanations, lists)
• Third-party contacts (may require notice)

sections.title: Responding Best Practices
rows: Leave examiner to guess what you sent

sections.title: Requesting Extensions
sections.content:
**When You Need More Time:**

1. Contact examiner BEFORE deadline
2. Explain reason for delay
3. Propose specific new deadline
4. Document the agreement in writing

**Reasonable Reasons:**
• Volume of documents requested
• Need to obtain from third parties
• Client unavailability
• Natural disaster or emergency

**Unreasonable:** Simply not wanting to comply

sections.title: Privileges and Protections
sections.content:
**Attorney-Client Privilege:**
• For communications with attorneys seeking legal advice
• Does NOT extend to EAs (limited exception below)

**Tax Practitioner Privilege (IRC §7525):**
• Limited privilege for federally authorized practitioners
• Only for non-criminal tax matters
• Does NOT apply to tax shelter advice
• Does NOT protect work papers

**Work Product Doctrine:**
• Protects materials prepared in anticipation of litigation
• May not apply during examination phase

sections.title: What Must Be Produced
sections.content:
**IRS Has Broad Authority:**

• All books and records relevant to tax liability
• Bank statements and cancelled checks
• Contracts and agreements
• Correspondence related to transactions
• Workpapers (generally not privileged)

**Exceptions:**
• Truly privileged communications
• Items not relevant to examination
• Items already in IRS possession

sections.title: ⚠️ Summons Power
sections.content:
**If You Refuse to Comply:**

IRS can issue a **Summons** (IRC §7602):
• Compels production of documents
• Compels testimony under oath
• Enforceable in federal court

**Failure to comply with summons:**
• Contempt of court
• Potential criminal penalties

**Better approach:** Negotiate scope, request extensions, but ultimately comply.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: IDRs are formal document requests - respond timely
Provide only what's specifically requested
Request extensions BEFORE deadline expires
Tax practitioner privilege is limited - doesn't cover work papers
Keep copies and document what you provide
IRS can issue summons if you don't comply
Transmittal letters create clear record of responses

knowledgeCheck.question: An IRS examiner issues a Form 4564, Information Document Request, to a taxpayer on March 1st, requesting bank statements from January through December of the tax year under examination. The IDR states a response is due by March 15th. The taxpayer's EA realizes they need an extension to gather all the documents. What is the BEST course of action for the EA?
options: Ignore the deadline and submit the documents when they are ready to avoid submitting incomplete information.
Immediately contact the examiner, explain the reason for the delay, propose a new specific deadline, and document the agreement in writing.
File Form 4868, Application for Automatic Extension of Time to File Individual Income Tax Return, to automatically extend the deadline.
Send a letter to the examiner acknowledging receipt of the IDR and stating that the documents will be provided as soon as possible, without specifying a new deadline.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The best course of action is to proactively contact the examiner to request an extension. Ignoring the deadline is unprofessional and could lead to penalties. Form 4868 is for extending the time to file a tax return, not responding to an IDR. Failing to specify a new deadline leaves the matter unresolved.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the importance of communication and documentation when dealing with the IRS.

reveal.question: Under what circumstances does the Tax Practitioner Privilege NOT apply?
reveal.answer: The Tax Practitioner Privilege does NOT apply to criminal tax matters or communications regarding tax shelters.

rows.label: Scope
values: Applies to all legal matters (criminal and non-criminal)
Limited to non-criminal tax matters

rows.label: Who it Protects
values: Communications with attorneys seeking legal advice
Communications with federally authorized practitioners (EAs, CPAs, Attorneys) to the extent they would be considered privileged if it were between a client and an attorney.

rows.label: Tax Shelters
values: Applies
Does NOT apply to tax shelters

rows.label: EAs Covered?
values: Generally no, unless acting under attorney supervision
Yes, in non-criminal tax matters (to the extent the communication would be considered privileged if it were between a client and an attorney)

title: Examination Closing Procedures
description: Understand how examinations conclude and client options at each stage

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: How an examination closes determines your client's next steps. Understanding the difference between agreed, unagreed, and no-change closures lets you advise clients on options and prepare for potential appeals.

sections.title: Three Examination Outcomes
rows: Sign Form 870, pay or set up payment plan

sections.title: No Change Closure
sections.content:
**Best Outcome:**

• Examiner found no adjustments
• Return accepted as filed
• Client receives "No Change" letter
• Case is closed

**Keep Records:**
• Retain documentation used in audit
• May be relevant if future years examined
• Shows positions were previously accepted

sections.title: Agreed Cases
sections.content:
**When Client Agrees with Adjustments:**

1. **Examiner prepares Form 4549** (Income Tax Examination Changes)
   • Shows proposed adjustments
   • Calculates additional tax, penalties, interest

2. **Client signs Form 870** (Waiver of Restrictions)
   • Waives requirement for statutory notice (90-day letter)
   • Allows immediate assessment
   • Interest continues until paid

3. **Assessment and Payment**
   • Tax assessed when Form 870 processed
   • Client has options for payment (lump sum, installment)

**Note:** Signing Form 870 generally cannot be undone!

sections.title: Unagreed Cases
sections.content:
**When Client Disagrees:**

1. **Request Manager Conference**
   • Meet with examiner's supervisor
   • May resolve issues at this level

2. **Receive 30-Day Letter**
   • Preliminary notice of proposed changes
   • Right to appeal within 30 days

3. **Choose Response:**
   • Request Appeals conference
   • Accept changes and pay
   • Do nothing (leads to 90-day letter)

4. **If Still Unagreed: Statutory Notice (90-Day Letter)**
   • Formal notice of deficiency
   • 90 days to petition Tax Court

sections.title: Form 870 vs. Form 870-AD
rows: Waives 90-day letter, cannot be reopened except for fraud
Closing agreement, prevents refund claim, prevents IRS reopening

sections.title: ⚠️ When NOT to Agree
sections.content:
**Consider rejecting settlement if:**

• Legal position is strong
• Penalty abatement is warranted but not offered
• Significant dollar amounts involved
• Examiner misunderstands facts or law
• Client willing to pursue Appeals or Tax Court

**Appeals** often reaches better results than examination level!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: No change = case closed with return accepted
Agreed = sign Form 870, waive right to 90-day letter
Unagreed = receive 30-day letter with appeal rights
Manager conference may resolve issues before appeal
Form 870 generally cannot be undone once signed
Form 870-AD at Appeals is a closing agreement
Appeals often achieves better results than examination

knowledgeCheck.question: An IRS examiner completes an audit of John Doe's tax return and determines that no adjustments are necessary. Which of the following actions will the IRS take?
options: The IRS will send John Doe a Form 4549 outlining the lack of adjustments.
The IRS will send John Doe a 'No Change' letter, and the case is closed.
The IRS will automatically examine John Doe's tax return for the subsequent year.
The IRS will require John Doe to sign Form 870.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When an audit results in no adjustments, the IRS sends a 'No Change' letter to the taxpayer, officially closing the case. Form 4549 is used when there *are* changes, and Form 870 is used when the taxpayer *agrees* to changes.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the outcome of the audit - no changes!

reveal.question: What does signing Form 870 signify?
reveal.answer: Signing Form 870 (Waiver of Restrictions) signifies that the taxpayer agrees with the proposed adjustments made by the IRS and waives their right to receive a statutory notice of deficiency (90-day letter), expediting the assessment and collection process.

rows.label: Timing
values: Requested during the examination phase, directly with the examiner's supervisor.
Requested after receiving the 30-day letter, as a formal appeal process.

rows.label: Purpose
values: To attempt to resolve disagreements at a supervisory level within the examining division.
To present your case to an independent appeals officer who is separate from the examining division.

rows.label: Formality
values: Less formal; an internal review process.
More formal; involves a separate appeals division.

title: 30-Day and 90-Day Letters
description: Understand IRS preliminary and statutory notices and response options

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The 30-day and 90-day letters are critical decision points in the examination process. Missing deadlines can eliminate options. Know what each letter means and how to respond.

sections.title: The 30-Day Letter
sections.content:
**Preliminary Notice:**

**What It Is:**
• IRS proposal of changes
• NOT a formal assessment
• Invitation to agree or appeal

**Contains:**
• Examination report (Form 4549)
• Explanation of changes
• Appeal rights information (Pub 5)

**Response Options (within 30 days):**
1. Sign and agree
2. Request Appeals conference
3. Provide additional information
4. Do nothing (leads to 90-day letter)

sections.title: The 90-Day Letter (Statutory Notice of Deficiency)
sections.content:
**The "Ticket to Tax Court":**

**What It Is:**
• Formal statutory notice required before assessment
• Also called "Notice of Deficiency"
• Certified mail to last known address

**Critical Deadline:**
• **90 days** from mailing to petition Tax Court
• **150 days** if addressed outside the US
• Missing deadline = lose Tax Court option!

**Effect:**
• Allows IRS to assess after 90 days
• Freezes assessment during petition period
• Starts prepayment forum jurisdiction

sections.title: Response Options to 90-Day Letter
sections.content:
**Within 90 Days:**

**Option 1: Petition Tax Court**
• Prepay is NOT required
• Get judicial review of proposed deficiency
• Must file timely petition

**Option 2: Agree and Pay**
• Sign and return agreement
• Pay or set up payment plan
• Case closed

**Option 3: Do Nothing**
• After 90 days, IRS assesses tax
• Collection process begins
• Can still contest by paying and filing refund claim

sections.title: Calculating the 90-Day Deadline
sections.content:
**The deadline is statutory - no extensions!**

**Calculate from mailing date:**
• Date on letter (postmark date for processing)
• Add 90 calendar days
• If 90th day is Saturday, Sunday, or holiday, next business day applies

**Example:**
• Letter dated January 15
• 90 days = April 15
• If April 15 is weekend, deadline is next Monday

**The date the petition is FILED (received) matters, not mailing date (usually)!**

**Electronic filing:** Available and encouraged

sections.title: ⚠️ Critical: Address Changes
sections.content:
**IRS sends notice to "last known address":**

• If client moved, may not receive 90-day letter!
• Letter sent to old address is still valid
• 90-day clock still runs

**Protect Clients:**
• File Form 8822 (change of address) promptly
• Consider USPS mail forwarding
• Monitor client addresses in IRS records

**Missed deadline = no Tax Court = must pay first then sue in refund forum!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 30-day letter is preliminary - invites agreement or appeal
90-day letter is statutory notice required for deficiency
90 days to petition Tax Court - NO EXTENSIONS
Missing 90-day deadline = lose prepayment forum
Sent to last known address - valid even if not received
Small case letter allows informal Appeals for ≤$25,000
Tax Court petition does NOT require prepayment

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is TRUE regarding a 30-day letter from the IRS?
options: It is a formal assessment of tax liability.
It must be sent via certified mail to the taxpayer's last known address.
It offers the taxpayer the opportunity to agree with proposed changes or appeal them.
Failure to respond within 30 days grants the IRS immediate authority to seize assets.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A 30-day letter is a preliminary notice proposing changes to a tax return, offering the taxpayer a chance to agree or appeal. It is not a formal assessment, and the IRS cannot immediately seize assets if the taxpayer doesn't respond. Certified mail is typically used for 90-day letters.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the purpose of each letter and what stage of the audit process they represent.

reveal.question: Which type of IRS letter is often referred to as the 'Ticket to Tax Court'?
reveal.answer: The 90-Day Letter (Statutory Notice of Deficiency) is known as the 'Ticket to Tax Court' because it is a prerequisite to petitioning the Tax Court. Receiving this letter gives the taxpayer the right to dispute the IRS's determination in Tax Court before paying the deficiency.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Preliminary Notice of Proposed Changes
Formal Statutory Notice of Deficiency

rows.label: Action Required
values: Respond within 30 days: Agree, Request Conference, or Do Nothing
Respond within 90 (or 150) days: Petition Tax Court, Agree and Pay, or Do Nothing

rows.label: Tax Court Access
values: Does NOT grant immediate access to Tax Court
Grants access to Tax Court if petitioned within the deadline

rows.label: Payment Required?
values: Payment is NOT immediately required
Payment is NOT required to petition Tax Court

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer residing in France receives a 90-day letter from the IRS via certified mail. How many days does the taxpayer have to petition the Tax Court?
options: 30 days
90 days
120 days
150 days
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Taxpayers addressed outside the United States are granted 150 days from the mailing date of the 90-day letter to petition the Tax Court.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the extended deadline for taxpayers residing outside the US.

title: IRS Appeals Process
description: Master the administrative appeals process for tax disputes

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: IRS Appeals resolves most tax disputes without litigation. Appeals officers have authority to settle cases based on hazards of litigation. Knowing how Appeals works lets you achieve better outcomes for clients.

sections.title: What is IRS Appeals?
sections.content:
**Independent Division of IRS:**

• Separate from Examination
• Mission is to resolve disputes fairly
• Settlement authority based on litigation hazards
• Informal process (not like court)

**Key Concept: Hazards of Litigation**
Appeals considers the likelihood that IRS would win in court. If IRS position is weak, they'll settle!

sections.title: How to Request Appeals
rows: Detailed statement of facts and law

sections.title: Written Protest Requirements
sections.content:
**For amounts over $25,000, include:**

1. Statement requesting Appeals conference
2. Your name, address, and contact information
3. Copy of the letter showing proposed changes
4. Tax periods or years involved
5. Statement of facts (in your words, not IRS's)
6. Discussion of applicable law
7. Argument why you disagree
8. Penalty of perjury statement

**Submit within the time stated in IRS letter** (usually 30 days)

sections.title: The Appeals Conference
sections.content:
**What to Expect:**

• Informal meeting (in-person, video, or phone)
• Present your case and arguments
• Appeals Officer considers both sides
• May request additional documentation
• Has settlement authority

**Preparation:**
• Organize legal arguments by issue
• Identify strengths and weaknesses
• Know your "walk away" point
• Prepare client for possible outcomes
• Bring documentation to support positions

sections.title: Settlement Strategies
sections.content:
**Effective Approaches:**

**1. Focus on hazards of litigation**
• Show where IRS position is legally weak
• Cite relevant case law

**2. Split the baby**
• Propose splitting contested amounts
• Shows reasonableness

**3. Trade issues**
• Concede weak issues
• Fight strong issues harder

**4. Penalty abatement**
• Often available even when tax sustained
• Reasonable cause arguments

**Appeals often achieves 20-50% reductions!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Ex Parte Communication Rules
sections.content:
**Appeals must remain independent:**

• Prohibited from certain communications with Exam
• Cannot discuss specific facts of case with examiner
• Can only discuss legal interpretations

**Why it matters:**
• You get fresh look at case
• Appeals isn't bound by examiner's views
• Different decision-maker may see it differently

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Appeals is independent from Examination
Settlement based on hazards of litigation
Small cases (≤$25K): Brief letter request
Larger cases: Formal written protest required
Focus arguments on where IRS is legally weak
Appeals often achieves significant reductions
Penalty abatement commonly available at Appeals

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following best describes the settlement authority of the IRS Appeals division?
options: Appeals only has the authority to review the case for errors and cannot negotiate a settlement.
Appeals has settlement authority based primarily on the hazards of litigation, considering the likelihood of the IRS prevailing in court.
Appeals has the same settlement authority as the Examination division, focusing on strict adherence to the tax code.
Appeals can only propose settlements that are pre-approved by the IRS Chief Counsel.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IRS Appeals division's settlement authority is primarily based on the hazards of litigation. They assess the strengths and weaknesses of the IRS's case and the taxpayer's arguments to determine a fair settlement, considering the potential outcome in court.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the Appeals division's mission - to resolve disputes fairly and efficiently.

reveal.question: List five required elements that must be included in a written protest to IRS Appeals when the total proposed adjustments exceed $25,000.
reveal.answer:
1. A statement requesting an Appeals conference.
2. Your name, address, and contact information.
3. A copy of the letter showing the proposed changes (e.g., the 30-day letter).
4. The tax periods or years involved.
5. A statement of facts (in your own words).
6. Discussion of applicable law.
7. Arguments supporting your position.

knowledgeCheck.question: During an Appeals conference, the Appeals Officer requests additional documentation from the taxpayer. What is the most appropriate course of action for the taxpayer?
options: Refuse to provide any additional documentation, as the initial protest should contain all necessary information.
Provide the requested documentation promptly and thoroughly, as it may assist the Appeals Officer in understanding the taxpayer's position and reaching a settlement.
Provide only a summary of the requested documentation to avoid overwhelming the Appeals Officer.
Demand that the Appeals Officer provide a written guarantee that the additional documentation will result in a favorable settlement.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Providing the requested documentation is the most appropriate action. The Appeals Officer's request indicates that they need more information to properly evaluate the case. Cooperation can facilitate a more informed and potentially favorable outcome.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the purpose of the Appeals conference: a collaborative effort to resolve disputes.

title: Return Filing Requirements
description: Master who must file and when for various return types

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Knowing who must file and when is fundamental! Penalties apply for late or missing returns. As a practitioner, ensuring clients meet filing obligations is one of your core responsibilities.

sections.title: Must File Regardless of Income
sections.content:
**Special filing requirements:**

• Self-employment income ≥ $400
• Social Security/Medicare tax on tips not reported to employer
• Advance Premium Tax Credit received
• Health Savings Account distributions
• Alternative minimum tax owed
• Household employment taxes owed
• Recapture taxes (first-time homebuyer, education)
• Write-in taxes

**Also file to claim refund or refundable credit!**

sections.title: Information Return Due Dates
sections.content:
**Forms 1099-NEC:** Due January 31 (to IRS and recipients)

**Forms 1099-MISC, 1099-INT, etc.:**
• To recipients: January 31
• To IRS: February 28 (paper) or March 31 (electronic)

**Forms W-2:**
• To employees: January 31
• To SSA: January 31

**E-file mandate:** Required for 10+ information returns

sections.title: Fiscal Year Due Dates
sections.content:
**For non-calendar year taxpayers:**

• Return due 15th day of 4th month after year end
• Extension adds 6 months (5 months for certain entities)

**Example: Fiscal year ending June 30:**
• Original due date: October 15
• Extended due date: April 15 (following year)

**Partnerships/S Corps with fiscal years:**
• Due 15th day of 3rd month after year end

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Individual thresholds vary by filing status and age
Must file with SE income ≥ $400 regardless of threshold
Partnerships/S Corps due March 15 (calendar year)
C Corps/Individuals due April 15 (calendar year)
1099-NEC due January 31 to both IRS and recipients
E-file required for 10+ information returns
File even below threshold if refund or credit available

knowledgeCheck.question: An individual has gross income of $1,000, consisting solely of self-employment income. They have no other income. Are they required to file a federal income tax return?
options: No, because their total income is below the standard deduction for a single filer.
Yes, because their self-employment income is $400 or more.
No, because self-employment income is not considered taxable income.
Yes, because all individuals with any income must file a tax return.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is B. Individuals with self-employment income of $400 or more are required to file a tax return, regardless of whether their total income exceeds the standard deduction. The $400 threshold triggers the requirement to pay self-employment taxes.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the special filing requirements, not just the standard deduction.

reveal.question: What is the due date for Form 1099-NEC, both to recipients and to the IRS?
reveal.answer: The due date for Form 1099-NEC is January 31st for both recipients and the IRS. This applies regardless of whether the form is filed on paper or electronically.

rows.label: Due Date
values: January 31
February 28
March 31

knowledgeCheck.question: A business needs to file 12 Forms 1099-MISC and 8 Forms 1099-NEC. Are they required to file electronically?
options: No, because they are only required to e-file if they file more than 250 of any single type of information return.
Yes, because they file more than 10 total information returns of any kind.
No, because they are not required to e-file unless their gross receipts exceed a certain amount.
Yes, because any business with more than 5 employees is required to e-file.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is B. The e-file mandate requires electronic filing if a business files 10 or more information returns of any type in aggregate. In this case, 12 + 8 = 20, which is over the threshold.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that the e-file threshold applies to all information returns combined, not each type separately.

title: Extensions and Estimated Taxes
description: Master extension procedures and estimated tax safe harbors

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Extensions give clients more time to file without penalty, but payment is still due on time. Understanding estimated tax requirements prevents underpayment penalties throughout the year.

sections.title: Extensions - Key Points
sections.content:
**Extension Extends TIME TO FILE, NOT TIME TO PAY!**

**Automatic upon filing:**
• No reason required
• IRS does not "approve"
• Just file timely and extension is granted

**Payment still due:**
• Estimate tax and pay with extension
• Failure to pay = penalty + interest
• Interest runs from original due date

**Auto-extension for overseas:**
• U.S. citizens/residents abroad: 2-month auto-extension
• Interest still accrues from original due date!

sections.title: Estimated Tax Requirements
sections.content:
**Who Must Pay Estimates:**

• Expected liability ≥ $1,000 after withholding and credits
• Self-employed individuals
• Investors with significant gains/dividends
• Retirees without adequate withholding

**Quarterly Due Dates (Calendar Year):**
• Q1: April 15
• Q2: June 15
• Q3: September 15
• Q4: January 15 (following year)

sections.title: Safe Harbor Rules
sections.content:
**Avoid Penalty by Paying Either:**

**Option 1: Current Year Method**
• 90% of current year's tax liability

**Option 2: Prior Year Method**
• 100% of prior year's tax liability, OR
• 110% if prior year AGI > $150,000 ($75,000 MFS)

**Prior year safe harbor is usually easier** - you know the amount!

**Requirements:**
• Prior year was 12-month tax year
• Prior year had a tax liability

sections.title: Annualized Income Method
sections.content:
**For Uneven Income Throughout Year:**

If income varies seasonally:
• Can annualize income each quarter
• Pay estimates based on income earned through period

**Form 2210, Schedule AI required**

**Useful for:**
• Seasonal businesses
• Capital gains realized in one quarter
• Bonuses received late in year

**Benefit:** Lower early estimates when income is low

sections.title: ⚠️ Underpayment Penalty
sections.content:
**Penalty if you don't meet safe harbors:**

• Interest-based penalty on underpayment
• Calculated quarter by quarter
• IRS or Form 2210 computes amount

**No penalty if:**
• Total tax less than $1,000
• You paid 100% (or 110%) of prior year
• You paid 90% of current year

**Increased withholding = estimated payments!**
Withholding is treated as paid evenly throughout the year.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Form 4868 extends filing to October 15 (individuals)
Extension is automatic - no approval needed
Payment still due by original due date
Estimate safe harbors: 90% current OR 100%/110% prior year
Quarterly estimates: April, June, September, January
Annualized income method for seasonal income variation
Increase W-4 withholding as alternative to estimates

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer Smith requests an automatic extension to file their 2023 tax return. The original due date was April 15, 2024. Smith estimates their tax liability to be $5,000, but only remits $2,000 with the extension request. Which of the following is TRUE?
options: Smith has successfully avoided all penalties because the extension was automatically granted.
Smith will only be assessed interest on the unpaid tax liability from October 15, 2024.
Smith will be assessed both a failure-to-pay penalty and interest on the unpaid tax liability, calculated from April 15, 2024.
Smith will be assessed a failure-to-file penalty, but not a failure-to-pay penalty, because they filed for an extension.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An extension to file does NOT extend the time to pay. Because Smith underpaid their estimated tax liability, they are subject to both the failure-to-pay penalty and interest, which accrues from the original due date of the return (April 15th).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the key point about extensions: it's only about the filing deadline, not the payment deadline.

reveal.question: What is the general threshold that triggers the requirement to pay estimated taxes?
reveal.answer: The estimated tax liability must be $1,000 or more after subtracting withholding and credits.

rows.label: Percentage of Liability
values: 90% of current year's tax liability
100% of prior year's tax liability (110% if prior year AGI exceeded $150,000, $75,000 if MFS)

rows.label: Advantage
values: Potentially lower payment if current year income is less than prior year
Easier to calculate as prior year's tax is known

rows.label: Risk
values: Requires accurately projecting current year income and deductions
May result in underpayment if current year income significantly exceeds prior year

title: Electronic Filing Requirements
description: Understand e-file mandates and preparer requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Electronic filing is now the standard. Most preparers must e-file, and clients expect it. Know the mandates, ERO responsibilities, and signature requirements.

sections.title: E-File Mandates
rows: Varies by form and filer size

sections.title: ERO Responsibilities
sections.content:
**Electronic Return Originator (ERO):**

**A preparer who e-files must:**
• Apply to be Authorized IRS e-file Provider
• Complete e-file application
• Pass suitability check
• Comply with Pub 1345 requirements

**ERO Duties:**
• Collect taxpayer signatures (Form 8879)
• Retain signed authorization 3 years
• Provide copy of return to taxpayer
• Correct rejected returns promptly

sections.title: Form 8879: IRS e-file Signature Authorization
sections.content:
**Required Before Transmitting Return:**

• Taxpayer (and spouse) must sign
• Authorizes ERO to enter PIN for e-signature
• Authorizes direct deposit/debit
• Must be signed AFTER return is complete

**Cannot transmit before obtaining signature!**

**Retain Form 8879 for 3 years** from due date or filing date, whichever is later.

sections.title: Electronic Signatures
sections.content:
**Self-Select PIN Program:**
• Taxpayer creates 5-digit PIN
• Used to sign e-filed return
• ERO enters on behalf of taxpayer (with authorization)

**Practitioner PIN:**
• Preparer enters taxpayer's prior-year AGI or PIN
• Creates signature for current return

**Remote Transactions:**
• IRS permits electronic signatures on Form 8879
• Specific requirements apply (email, portal, etc.)
• Made permanent after COVID-era temporary rules

sections.title: Rejected Returns
sections.content:
**If E-File Is Rejected:**

1. Notify taxpayer within 24 hours
2. Take reasonable steps to fix error
3. Re-transmit corrected return

**Common Rejection Reasons:**
• Duplicate SSN (already filed)
• AGI/PIN mismatch
• Incorrect dependent SSN
• Name/SSN mismatch with SSA records

**If can't be fixed:** May file paper return with rejection code

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Preparers filing 11+ returns must e-file
Must be Authorized IRS e-file Provider
Form 8879 required before transmitting
Retain Form 8879 for 3 years
Electronic signatures now permitted on 8879
Fix and re-transmit rejected returns within 24 hours
Paper filing allowed if e-file rejected and uncorrectable

knowledgeCheck.question: An Electronic Return Originator (ERO) completes a client's tax return on March 10th, 2024, and e-files it on March 12th, 2024. The client signed Form 8879 on March 9th, 2024. Which of the following is true regarding the ERO's actions?
options: The ERO acted appropriately; the signature was obtained before the return was filed.
The ERO acted inappropriately; Form 8879 must be signed on or after the date the return is completed.
The ERO acted appropriately; Form 8879 only needs to be retained for 2 years.
The ERO acted inappropriately; the ERO should have used the Practitioner PIN method instead.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The ERO acted inappropriately. Form 8879 must be signed by the taxpayer(s) AFTER the tax return is completed. In this case, the return was completed on March 10th, but the form was signed on March 9th.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider when the return was completed vs. when the form was signed.

reveal.question: List three key responsibilities of an Electronic Return Originator (ERO).
reveal.answer:
1. Apply to be an Authorized IRS e-file Provider.
2. Collect taxpayer signatures (Form 8879).
3. Retain signed authorization (Form 8879) for 3 years from the due date or filing date, whichever is later.
4. Comply with Pub 1345 requirements.

rows.label: PIN Creation
values: Taxpayer creates a 5-digit PIN.
Preparer uses taxpayer's prior-year AGI or PIN to create a signature.

rows.label: Taxpayer Involvement
values: Taxpayer directly chooses and provides the PIN.
Taxpayer does not directly create a PIN for the current year; prior-year data is used.

rows.label: Form 8879 Required?
values: Yes, Form 8879 is required.
Yes, Form 8879 is required.

title: Non-Filer Procedures
description: Handle clients who haven't filed required returns

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Many potential clients walk in with years of unfiled returns. Knowing how to bring them into compliance, deal with IRS notices, and challenge substitute returns is essential for building a tax practice.

sections.title: Substitute for Return (SFR)
sections.content:
**What is an SFR?**

If you don't file, IRS can prepare a return FOR you:
• Uses available information (W-2s, 1099s)
• Files as Single or MFS (least favorable)
• No itemized deductions
• No exemptions/credits
• Almost always OVERSTATES tax

**The SFR is assessed, and collection begins!**

sections.title: SFR Process
rows: Petition Tax Court OR file actual return

sections.title: Filing Delinquent Returns
sections.content:
**To Replace an SFR:**

1. Prepare accurate return for the year(s)
2. File with IRS (by mail if e-file rejected)
3. Include all applicable deductions and credits
4. Show correct tax liability

**IRS Processing:**
• Reviews filed return vs. SFR
• Adjusts assessment if return accepted
• May audit filed return

**Time limit:** Generally none to file correcting return, but refunds expire after 3 years from original due date

sections.title: Penalty Relief for Non-Filers
sections.content:
**First Time Abatement:**
• Available if prior 3 years clean
• Covers failure-to-file and failure-to-pay
• Request when filing delinquent returns

**Reasonable Cause:**
• Medical issues, natural disasters
• Death in family
• Reliance on professional
• Document thoroughly!

**File extension for future years** even if not ready to file the return

sections.title: Voluntary Disclosure Practice
sections.content:
**For Serious Non-Compliance:**

**Criminal Investigation Voluntary Disclosure:**
• Pre-clearance required
• Before IRS initiates investigation
• May avoid criminal prosecution
• Civil penalties still apply

**Offshore Voluntary Disclosure:**
• For unreported foreign income/accounts
• Specific procedures apply
• Better than getting caught!

**Consult with specialists** for serious non-filer cases

sections.title: ⚠️ Practice Tips
sections.content:
**Working with Non-Filers:**

• Get ALL prior returns filed before current year
• IRS prioritizes compliance - show good faith
• Request penalty abatement in writing
• Consider Installment Agreement for amounts owed
• Watch refund statutes - they expire!

**Document everything** - these cases often have collection issues too

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SFR is IRS-prepared return - usually wrong and overstated
SFR uses single/MFS, no deductions, no credits
Can replace SFR by filing accurate return
First Time Abatement available if prior 3 years clean
Refund expires 3 years from original due date
Get all delinquent years filed for compliance
Serious cases may need criminal defense counsel

knowledgeCheck.question: The IRS prepares a Substitute for Return (SFR) when a taxpayer fails to file their income tax return. Which of the following is TRUE regarding an SFR?
options: An SFR allows the taxpayer to claim itemized deductions and all applicable credits.
The IRS typically files the SFR using the most favorable filing status for the taxpayer.
An SFR is based on information available to the IRS, such as W-2s and 1099s, and generally overstates the tax liability.
Once an SFR is filed, the taxpayer is no longer able to file their own return for that tax year.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IRS prepares an SFR using available information (W-2s, 1099s) and typically files as Single or MFS (least favorable). It does NOT allow for itemized deductions or credits, and it almost always overstates the tax liability. Taxpayers CAN file their own return to replace the SFR.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider how the IRS would act in its own best interest when creating an SFR.

reveal.question: What are the key steps to take when replacing an SFR with an accurately prepared return?
reveal.answer:
1. Prepare an accurate return for the year(s) in question, including all applicable deductions and credits.
2. File the return with the IRS (by mail if e-file is rejected).
3. Ensure the return shows the correct tax liability.

rows.label: Eligibility
values: Requires a clean compliance history for the prior 3 years (no penalties).
Based on specific circumstances that prevented timely filing or payment.

rows.label: Examples
values: N/A - Based purely on prior compliance.
Medical issues, natural disasters, death in family, reliance on a professional.

rows.label: Documentation
values: Generally less documentation required.
Requires thorough documentation to support the reason for failing to file or pay on time.

title: Penalties and Interest
description: Master common penalties, interest calculations, and abatement strategies

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Penalties and interest can exceed the original tax! Knowing the penalty structure, how interest compounds, and strategies for abatement helps you protect clients and manage their liabilities.

sections.title: Failure to File vs. Failure to Pay
sections.content:
**Failure to File (FTF):**
• 5% per month (or part of month)
• Maximum 25%
• Minimum: lesser of $510 or 100% of tax (if 60+ days late)

**Failure to Pay (FTP):**
• 0.5% per month
• Maximum 25%
• Increases to 1%/month after notice demand
• Reduces to 0.25% if on installment agreement

**When Both Apply:**
FTF reduced by FTP for same month (net 4.5%/month + 0.5%/month = 5%)

sections.title: Accuracy-Related Penalties
sections.content:
**20% Penalty for:**

• **Negligence:** Lack of reasonable attempt to comply
• **Disregard:** Careless, reckless, or intentional disregard of rules
• **Substantial Understatement:**
  - Individuals: > $5,000 OR > 10% of correct tax
  - Corporations: > $10 million OR > 10% of correct tax
• **Substantial Valuation Misstatement:** Overstated value by 150%+

**Defense:** Reasonable cause and good faith

sections.title: Interest on Underpayments
sections.content:
**Interest Rate:**
• Federal short-term rate + 3%
• Compounded daily
• Adjusted quarterly
• Typically 7-8% (varies by economy)

**Interest Cannot Be Abated!**
• Only exceptions: IRS error causing delay
• Even with penalty abatement, interest continues

**Interest runs from:**
• Due date of return (or extended due date for payment)
• Assessment date for some penalties

sections.title: Penalty Abatement Strategies
rows: 3 prior years clean, all returns filed

sections.title: Requesting Abatement
sections.content:
**First Time Abatement (FTA):**
• Request by phone (for simple cases)
• Write letter citing FTA administrative waiver
• Applies to FTF and FTP for one year

**Reasonable Cause:**
• Written request explaining circumstances
• Document ordinary business care
• Explain what caused non-compliance
• Show how you tried to comply

**Form 843** for formal refund claim including penalty abatement

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: FTF = 5%/month (max 25%); FTP = 0.5%/month (max 25%)
Combined can reach 47.5% plus interest
Accuracy penalty = 20% for negligence or understatement
Interest cannot be abated (except IRS error)
First Time Abatement if 3 prior years clean
Reasonable cause requires showing ordinary business care
Request abatement in writing with documentation

sections.title: Failure to File vs. Failure to Pay Penalty

knowledgeCheck.question: Taxpayer A filed their 2022 return on November 15, 2023. The return was due April 15, 2023, and showed a tax liability of $10,000. Taxpayer A paid $6,000 on April 15, 2023, and the remaining $4,000 with the filing on November 15, 2023. Ignoring interest, what is the *total* penalty for both Failure to File and Failure to Pay?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Failure to File penalty is 5% per month, capped at 25%. Since the return was filed 7 months late (April 15 to Nov 15), the penalty is 25% of the unpaid tax ($10,000), or $2,500. The Failure to Pay penalty is 0.5% per month, capped at 25%. Since $4,000 was paid 7 months late, the penalty is 0.5% * 7 * $4,000 = $140. However, since the question asks for the *total* penalty and it's capped at 25% for FTP, we calculate .25 * $4,000 = $1,000. We then need to calculate the penalties separately. FTF: 25% * $10,000 = $2500. FTP: 0.5% * 7 months * $4,000 = $140. Total Penalty: $2500 (FTF) + $140(FTP) = $2640, however, since they paid $6,000 on time, the FTF penalty is only on the $4,000, so 25% * $4,000 = $1,000. The FTP is calculated on $4,000, so $140. $1,000 + $140 = $1,140. The FTF penalty is capped at 25% of the unpaid tax. The unpaid tax is $4,000. 25% of $4,000 is $1,000. The FTP penalty is 0.5% per month, capped at 25%. 0.5% * 7 months = 3.5%. 3.5% of $4,000 is $140. The total penalty is $1,000 + $140 = $1,140. The Failure to File penalty is 5% per month, capped at 25% of the unpaid tax. Since the return was filed 7 months late, the penalty is 25% of the unpaid tax ($10,000), or $2,500. The Failure to Pay penalty is 0.5% per month, capped at 25%. Since $4,000 was paid 7 months late, the penalty is 0.5% * 7 * $4,000 = $140. Total Penalty is $2,500 + $140 = $2,640. However, since the FTF penalty is calculated on the amount of tax that remains unpaid, it is 25% of $4,000 = $1,000. Total Penalty is $1,000 + $140 = $1,140.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the maximum penalties and that penalties are based on unpaid tax.

reveal.question: Under what circumstances can interest on an underpayment be abated?
reveal.answer: Interest can *only* be abated if the IRS made an error or caused a delay in payment. Even if penalties are abated, interest continues to accrue.

rows.label: Monthly Rate
values: 5% per month (or part of month)
0.5% per month (or part of month)

rows.label: Increase After Notice
values: N/A
Increases to 1%/month after notice demand

rows.label: Reduction with Installment Agreement
values: N/A
Reduces to 0.25% if on installment agreement

rows.label: Minimum Penalty (if 60+ days late)
values: Lesser of $510 or 100% of tax
N/A

knowledgeCheck.question: An individual's tax return shows a correct tax liability of $60,000, but the return as filed reported a tax liability of $52,000. What is the accuracy-related penalty for substantial understatement?
options: No penalty, as the understatement is less than $5,000.
No penalty, as the understatement is less than 10% of the correct tax liability.
20% of the $8,000 understatement.
20% of the correct tax liability of $60,000.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The understatement is $8,000 ($60,000 - $52,000). Since this is greater than both $5,000 AND 10% of the correct tax liability ($60,000 * 10% = $6,000), the substantial understatement penalty applies. The penalty is 20% of the understatement, or 20% of $8,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the 'AND' condition for substantial understatement.

title: Audit Process: Selection and Types
description: Understanding how returns are selected for examination and the different types of audits.

sections.title: Audit Selection Methods
sections.content:
**DIF System (Discriminant Function):** Computer scoring (higher score = higher audit probability). Uses algorithms to find anomalies.
**UIDIF (Unreported Income DIF):** Scores based on potential for unreported income.
**Information Matching:** 1099s/W-2s don't match return.
**Related Examinations:** Often happens when a business partner or investor is audited.

sections.title: Types of Examinations
rows: Simple issues: Missing 1099, Math errors, Charity substantiation
At IRS office. Interview with Tax Compliance Officer.
Small business Sch C, Rental expenses, Moving expenses
At taxpayer home/business. Revenue Agent involved.
Complex specific issues, Inventory, Gross receipts, large corporations

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Most audits are limited to specific items (Correspondence).
Field audits are the most comprehensive.
DIF score is the primary selection method.
Representation is allowed at all levels.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements BEST describes the primary purpose of the IRS's Discriminant Function (DIF) system?
options: To randomly select tax returns for audit to ensure fairness.
To identify tax returns with the highest probability of containing errors or unreported income.
To provide taxpayers with feedback on how to improve their tax compliance.
To track the performance of IRS auditors.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The DIF system uses computer algorithms to score tax returns based on the likelihood of errors or unreported income. Returns with higher DIF scores are more likely to be selected for audit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what the IRS is trying to achieve when selecting returns for audit.

reveal.question: What is the specific purpose of the Unreported Income DIF (UIDIF) system?
reveal.answer: The UIDIF system specifically scores tax returns based on their potential for unreported income, helping the IRS identify returns where income may have been omitted or underreported.

rows.label: Location
values: Conducted via mail or phone.
Taxpayer visits an IRS office.
IRS agent visits the taxpayer's home or place of business.

rows.label: Complexity
values: Typically involves simple issues and limited scope.
More complex than correspondence audits, may require documentation.
Most complex type, involving extensive review of records and operations.

rows.label: Typical Issues
values: Math errors, missing information, or discrepancies in reported income.
Deductions, credits, or income items that require further clarification.
Business expenses, valuation of assets, or complex tax shelters.

title: Audit Representation and Taxpayer Rights
description: Rights of taxpayers during examination and role of the representative.

sections.title: Taxpayer Bill of Rights (TBOR)
items: The Right to Be Informed
The Right to Quality Service
The Right to Pay No More than the Correct Amount of Tax
The Right to Challenge the IRS's Position and Be Heard
The Right to Appeal an IRS Decision in an Independent Forum
The Right to Finality
The Right to Privacy
The Right to Confidentiality
The Right to Retain Representation
The Right to a Fair and Just Tax System

sections.title: Role of Representative
sections.content:
**Form 2848 (POA):** Required to represent.
**Benefits:**
• IRS contacts YOU, not the client.
• You can sign agreements (if authorized).
• You attend interviews (client generally not required if you are there).

**Bypassing the Rep:** IRS can bypass if you are causing unreasonable delays or hindering the examination.

sections.title: Assessment Statute (ASED)
sections.content:
Generally **3 years** from later of due date or filing date.
**6 Years:** If >25% gross income omitted.
**Unlimited:** Fraud, or no return filed.

**Extension (Form 872):** IRS may ask to extend statute to finish audit. Taxpayer can refuse (IRS will likely issue Notice of Deficiency immediately) or limit scope.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following actions is generally NOT permitted for a representative authorized via Form 2848, Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative?
options: Receiving copies of IRS notices on behalf of the taxpayer.
Signing a closing agreement regarding the taxpayer's liability, if specifically authorized.
Submitting a written protest to the IRS Appeals Office.
Endorsing and cashing the taxpayer's refund check.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While a Form 2848 allows a representative to receive notices, sign agreements (if specifically authorized), and submit protests, it does NOT authorize the representative to endorse and cash the taxpayer's refund check. Refund checks are payable to the taxpayer only.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider who the refund check is made out to.

reveal.question: What is the general statute of limitations for the IRS to assess additional tax?
reveal.answer: Generally, the IRS has 3 years from the later of the due date of the return or the date the return was filed to assess additional tax.

sections.title: Assessment Statute of Limitations: Extended Periods

rows.label: Statute of Limitations
values: 3 years
6 years
Unlimited

rows.label: Triggering Event
values: Standard assessment period
Omission of >25% of gross income
Intentional misrepresentation or failure to file

title: The Appeals Process
description: From 30-Day Letter to Appeals Conference.

sections.title: 30-Day Letter (Preliminary Notice)
sections.content:
Issued after audit if unagreed.
**Options:**
1. Sign agreement (Form 870) - Give up appeal rights.
2. Do nothing - Wait for 90-Day Letter.
3. Request Appeals Conference (within 30 days).

sections.title: How to Appeal
rows: Small Case Request (Form 12203 or simple written request). No formal protest needed.
Formal Written Protest required. Must detail facts, law, and arguments.

sections.title: The Appeals Mission
sections.content: To resolve tax controversies, without litigation, on a basis which is fair and impartial to both the Government and the taxpayer. Appeals Officers can settle based on 'Hazards of Litigation'.

knowledgeCheck.question: After receiving a 30-Day Letter following an audit, a taxpayer disagrees with the proposed adjustments but wishes to retain their appeal rights within the IRS. Which of the following actions should the taxpayer take?
options: Sign Form 870, waiving their right to appeal and agreeing to the proposed adjustments.
Do nothing and wait for the issuance of a 90-Day Letter.
Request an Appeals conference within 30 days of the date of the 30-Day Letter.
File a lawsuit in U.S. Tax Court immediately.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Requesting an Appeals conference within 30 days allows the taxpayer to present their case to the IRS Office of Appeals and potentially resolve the issue without going to court. Signing Form 870 waives appeal rights, doing nothing results in a 90-Day Letter and eventual tax court, and a lawsuit cannot be filed until after the 90-day letter is issued.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which option allows for further discussion with the IRS without immediately escalating the situation.

reveal.question: What is the primary consequence of signing Form 870 after receiving a 30-Day Letter?
reveal.answer: Signing Form 870 signifies agreement with the proposed tax adjustments and waives the taxpayer's right to appeal those adjustments within the IRS or in U.S. Tax Court. The assessment and collection of the tax will proceed.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Preliminary notice of proposed adjustments after an audit, offering an opportunity to appeal within the IRS.
Final notice that the IRS intends to assess a deficiency; offers the opportunity to petition the Tax Court.

rows.label: Taxpayer Options
values: Agree (Form 870), Do Nothing (leads to 90-day letter), Request Appeals Conference
Petition the U.S. Tax Court within 90 days, or pay the deficiency.

rows.label: Timing
values: Issued before the 90-Day Letter.
Issued after the 30-Day Letter process (or if no response to 30-day letter)

title: Going to Court: Tax Court vs Others
description: Litigation options when administrative appeals fail.

sections.title: Notice of Deficiency (90-Day Letter)
sections.content:
**The Ticket to Tax Court.**
Issued if 30-day letter ignored or Appeals fails.
**Strict Deadline:** 90 days (150 if outside US) from date of letter to file petition. Cannot be extended.

sections.title: Small Tax Case Procedure (Tax Court)
sections.content:
**Limit:** $50,000 or less per tax year.
**Pros:** Simplified, less formal, faster, cheaper.
**Cons:** Decision is FINAL. Cannot be appealed.

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer residing in Canada receives a Notice of Deficiency on March 1st. What is the last day they can file a petition with the Tax Court?
options: May 29th
July 29th
June 29th
August 1st
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Taxpayers outside the US have 150 days from the date of the Notice of Deficiency to file a petition with the Tax Court. March 1st + 150 days = July 29th.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the extended deadline for taxpayers residing outside the US.

reveal.question: What is the maximum amount in dispute, per tax year, that qualifies a case for the Small Tax Case procedure in Tax Court?
reveal.answer: $50,000. Cases exceeding this limit are not eligible for the simplified procedures of the Small Tax Case division.

rows.label: Payment Required Before Filing
values: No
Yes

rows.label: Jury Trial Available
values: No
Yes

rows.label: Expertise
values: Tax Law
General Law

knowledgeCheck.question: True or False: A taxpayer who loses a case under the Small Tax Case procedures in Tax Court can appeal the decision to a higher court.
options: True
False
knowledgeCheck.explanation: False. One of the major disadvantages of the Small Tax Case procedure is that the Tax Court's decision is final and cannot be appealed.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the trade-offs of using the Small Tax Case procedure.

title: Collections: Installment Agreements
description: Options for taxpayers who cannot pay in full immediately.

sections.title: Setting up an Agreement
sections.content:
Form 9465 used to request. Fees apply (lower for Direct Debit).
**Note:** Interest and Failure to Pay penalties continue to accrue (penalty rate cut in half usually).

sections.title: Partial Payment Installment Agreement (PPIA)
sections.content: Taxpayer pays what they can afford until CSED expires. Remainder is uncollected. Requires full financial disclosure (Form 433) and periodic review (every 2 years).

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements regarding installment agreements (IAs) is CORRECT?
options: Requesting an IA automatically suspends the accrual of interest and penalties.
Form 9465 is used to request an IA, and a lower fee generally applies if payments are made via direct debit.
Partial Payment Installment Agreements (PPIAs) do not require financial disclosure if the taxpayer's total tax liability is below $10,000.
Once an IA is approved, the failure-to-pay penalty is permanently waived.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Form 9465 is the correct form. Interest and failure-to-pay penalties continue to accrue even with an IA, and PPIAs require full financial disclosure (Form 433). The failure-to-pay penalty is cut in half, not waived.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what steps are required to get an IA, and what happens to penalties and interest.

reveal.question: How often is a Partial Payment Installment Agreement (PPIA) typically reviewed by the IRS?
reveal.answer: A PPIA is generally reviewed every 2 years. This ensures the taxpayer's financial situation is still accurately reflected and that the payment arrangement remains appropriate.

rows.label: Financial Disclosure
values: Generally Not Required
Required (Form 433)

rows.label: Taxpayer Payment
values: Full payment within the agreement terms
Taxpayer pays what they can afford until CSED

rows.label: CSED Impact
values: Full liability is paid before CSED
Remainder of liability may be uncollectible after CSED

title: Collections: Offer in Compromise (OIC)
description: Resolving tax liability for less than the full amount owed.

sections.title: What is an OIC?
sections.content: An agreement between a taxpayer and the IRS that resolves the taxpayer's tax liability. The IRS has the authority to settle, or compromise, federal tax liabilities by accepting less than full payment under certain circumstances.

sections.title: Three Bases for OIC
sections.content:
1. **Doubt as to Liability:** Genuine dispute exists as to the existence or amount.
2. **Doubt as to Collectibility:** Taxpayer's receipts + equity (RCP) < Tax Owed. (Most common).
3. **Effective Tax Administration (ETA):** Owe it, can pay it, but would cause exceptional hardship or be unfair.

sections.title: The Math: Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP)
sections.content:
**RCP = (Net Equity in Assets) + (Future Disposable Income)**

If IRS calculates RCP > Offer Amount, they will reject.
**Disposable Income Multiplier:**
• Cash Offer (paid in 5 months): Monthly Income x 12
• Periodic Payment (6-24 months): Monthly Income x 24

sections.title: Process
items: File Form 656 and Form 433-A (OIC).
Pay application fee and initial payment (unless Low Income Certification).
While pending: Collection paused, CSED suspended.
If rejected: Can appeal within 30 days.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios BEST describes a situation where an Offer in Compromise (OIC) would be submitted based on Doubt as to Collectibility?
options: The taxpayer believes the IRS made a mathematical error in calculating their tax liability.
The taxpayer lacks sufficient assets and future income to fully pay the assessed tax liability.
The taxpayer agrees with the assessed tax liability but believes that paying it would create an undue economic hardship due to extraordinary circumstances.
The taxpayer is unsure whether they are legally obligated to pay the assessed tax liability.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Doubt as to Collectibility arises when the taxpayer's assets and future income are insufficient to cover the tax debt. The other options describe Doubt as to Liability or Effective Tax Administration.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the taxpayer's ability to pay, not the validity of the tax itself or potential hardship.

reveal.question: What is the form number required to submit an Offer in Compromise (OIC) to the IRS?
reveal.answer: Form 656 is the form used to submit an Offer in Compromise.

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer proposes an Offer in Compromise (OIC) of $25,000. The IRS calculates the taxpayer's Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP) to be $30,000. Assuming all other OIC requirements are met, what is the MOST likely outcome of the OIC?
options: The OIC will be accepted because the taxpayer is offering a substantial amount.
The OIC will be rejected because the RCP exceeds the offer amount.
The OIC will be accepted if the taxpayer agrees to increase the offer to $30,000.
The OIC will be held in suspense pending further investigation.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IRS generally rejects OICs when the Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP) exceeds the offer amount. They expect to collect at least the amount they believe the taxpayer can realistically pay.
knowledgeCheck.hint: The IRS wants to maximize tax collection; compare the offer to the RCP.

rows.label: Key Factor
values: Validity of the tax assessment.
Taxpayer's financial capacity.
Exceptional circumstances and fairness.

title: Liens and Levies
description: IRS enforcement tools and how to release them.

sections.title: Federal Tax Lien
sections.content:
**Lien:** Legal claim against property to secure payment.
**Levy:** Actual seizure of property/funds.

**Notice of Federal Tax Lien (NFTL):** Used to establish priority against other creditors. Public record. Harmful to credit.

sections.title: Releasing a Lien
items: Pay in full (automatic release within 30 days).
Discharge of Property: Removing lien from specific property (e.g., to sell a house).
Subordination: Letting another creditor move ahead of IRS (e.g., to refinance).
Withdrawal: Removing the public notice (as if it never happened) - e.g., if filed in error or entered Direct Debit IA.

sections.title: Levy and Seizure
sections.content:
**Wage Garnishment:** Continuous levy until released. Exempt amount is very low (based on standard deduction).
**Bank Levy:** One-time seizure of funds in account on that day. 21-day holding period before bank sends money (time to release).

sections.title: Collection Due Process (CDP)
sections.content: Taxpayer must receive 'Final Notice of Intent to Levy' and right to CDP hearing 30 days before levy (except Jeopardy/State Refund).

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements BEST describes the difference between a federal tax lien and a levy?
options: A lien is the legal right to seize property, while a levy is the actual seizure of property.
A lien is a public notice of debt, while a levy is a private agreement with the IRS.
A lien is a one-time event, while a levy is a continuous action.
A lien is used to establish payment plans, while a levy is used when payment plans fail.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A federal tax lien is a legal claim against your property to secure payment of a tax debt. A levy is the actual seizure of your property to satisfy that debt. Therefore, option A is the most accurate description.
